2cf6d02e-5c41-40c6-8dea-2c5d9756b9c1.name: FDA
2cf6d02e-5c41-40c6-8dea-2c5d9756b9c1.parent: US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
keyOffices: Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition (CFSAN)
2cf6d02e-5c41-40c6-8dea-2c5d9756b9c1.description:
FDA regulates roughly 80% of the US food supply by value: packaged/processed foods, produce, seafood, dairy and shell eggs, bottled water, dietary supplements, wine under 7% ABV, and beer not made with both malted barley and hops.

Its authority is broader in scope but lighter-touch in presence than USDA FSIS's: rather than stationing an inspector at every regulated plant continuously, FSMA directs FDA to inspect high-risk facilities at least every 3 years and other facilities at least every 5 years, using registration and risk categorization (not physical presence) as the primary control. The practical consequence is that most day-to-day compliance assurance for FDA-regulated food comes from the facility's own PCHF-mandated Food Safety Plan and PCQI oversight, verified periodically by FDA inspection, rather than continuous federal oversight.

FDA's recall authority is mandatory under FSMA Section 423 for FDA-regulated food generally, with a separate, older mandatory mechanism specifically for infant formula (21 CFR Part 107) that predates and works differently from Section 423.

aac69997-d9a2-4428-860f-1d8813e9ba5a.name: USDA FSIS
aac69997-d9a2-4428-860f-1d8813e9ba5a.fullName: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service
aac69997-d9a2-4428-860f-1d8813e9ba5a.description:
FSIS regulates meat, poultry, and processed egg products (not shell eggs, which fall to FDA). Its inspection model is the inverse of FDA's: rather than periodic risk-based visits, federal law requires a USDA inspector physically present during all hours slaughter is conducted (continuous, shift-by-shift inspection), and inspectors visit processing-only plants (further processing, not slaughter) at least once per operating day. This reflects FMIA/PPIA's older, presence-based statutory design rather than FSMA's newer risk-based approach.

Since 2013/2023 rule changes, most meat/poultry labels are self-certified under generic label approval rather than requiring FSIS pre-approval, which now applies only to special claims (e.g. "organic," "natural").

FSIS has no independent authority to compel a recall — every FSIS recall is voluntary, negotiated with the firm; if a firm refuses, FSIS's only lever is referring the matter to the Department of Justice for court-ordered detention or seizure of the product, a materially weaker enforcement posture than FDA's direct mandatory-recall authority under FSMA Section 423.

c8b293a1-17bc-42b2-b023-7d0c6dba905d.name: USDA AMS
c8b293a1-17bc-42b2-b023-7d0c6dba905d.summary: Administers voluntary, fee-based grading and marketing standards (e.g., USDA Grade A, USDA Choice) and the National Organic Program (NOP), which certifies organic farms and handlers. Also administers the National Bioengineered Food Disclosure Standard (NBFDS).

49089417-12e3-4123-8005-9e169c1ed9ce.name: EPA
49089417-12e3-4123-8005-9e169c1ed9ce.summary: Sets maximum pesticide residue tolerances on food and feed under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (enforced by FDA/USDA), regulates food-contact sanitizers as antimicrobial pesticides under FIFRA (with tolerance exemptions cross-referenced to FDA rules), and sets drinking water standards (Safe Drinking Water Act) that affect food manufacturing water quality.

fa6a738b-947e-4ff9-a3cf-9b076e39a7a7.name: TTB
fa6a738b-947e-4ff9-a3cf-9b076e39a7a7.fullName: Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau
fa6a738b-947e-4ff9-a3cf-9b076e39a7a7.summary: Regulates labeling and production of beer made with both malted barley and hops, wine at 7% ABV or greater, and spirits. Wine under 7% ABV and other fermented malt beverages fall to FDA instead.

3c72b77c-84b1-4fc5-88bc-7a97193e2ffe.name: FTC
3c72b77c-84b1-4fc5-88bc-7a97193e2ffe.summary: Regulates food advertising and marketing claims (as distinct from FDA, which regulates label content itself), per a long-standing FTC/FDA liaison agreement (originally 1971) dividing jurisdiction. Coordinates with FDA on misleading claims.

57ebd4ad-028d-49a4-9840-805e858231d7.name: State & Local Agencies
57ebd4ad-028d-49a4-9840-805e858231d7.fullName: State departments of agriculture/health and local health departments
57ebd4ad-028d-49a4-9840-805e858231d7.summary: Can be stricter than federal standards but not laxer. Responsible for retail/restaurant licensing and inspection (using the FDA Food Code — adopted in some form by ~97% of states/territories — as a model), some intrastate manufacturing licensing, weights-and-measures verification (against NIST Handbook 44 as the federal reference model), and cottage food laws.

2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.name: CDC
2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.fullName: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.parent: US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
2ba049e2-d8dd-4172-b13b-5830326ab4ad.summary: Tracks and investigates foodborne illness outbreaks; estimates the annual US burden of foodborne disease at ~48M illnesses, ~128,000 hospitalizations, and ~3,000 deaths — CDC's current published headline estimate, though the methodology behind it dates to 2011 (Scallan et al.) rather than a recent study. A narrower, more recent CDC estimate (2025) covers just 7 major pathogens for 2019 (~9.9M illnesses, ~53,300 hospitalizations, 931 deaths) and does not replace the broader 48M figure, which also covers unspecified agents.

2ba804d3-9dc2-4848-94d1-f48c41ad9703.name: GS1
2ba804d3-9dc2-4848-94d1-f48c41ad9703.summary: Maintains the global identification standards (GTIN, GLN, SSCC, EPCIS) underlying barcodes, EDI, and traceability systems across industries, including food. Not a government regulator.

3b121647-ec9f-444c-9084-8a6637d357da.name: GFSI
3b121647-ec9f-444c-9084-8a6637d357da.summary: Benchmarking body (not itself an auditing scheme) that recognizes third-party food safety certification schemes (SQF, BRC, FSSC 22000, IFS) as meeting an internationally agreed standard. Major retailers require GFSI-recognized certification as a commercial condition of doing business.

425f7abc-b53d-413d-b5ba-abfa92280e1c.name: OSHA
425f7abc-b53d-413d-b5ba-abfa92280e1c.summary: Sets and enforces workplace safety standards directly applicable to food manufacturing plants: the General Duty Clause (requiring a workplace free of recognized hazards), machine guarding, lockout/tagout, personal protective equipment, and Process Safety Management for facilities with large anhydrous ammonia refrigeration inventories (common in cold storage/finished-goods warehousing). Distinct from FDA/USDA FSIS, which regulate product safety rather than worker safety.

9c1e2f3a-4b5c-4d6e-8f7a-1b2c3d4e5f6a.name: CBP
9c1e2f3a-4b5c-4d6e-8f7a-1b2c3d4e5f6a.summary: Processes entry filings for imported goods, including FDA-regulated food, and refers those entries to FDA for admissibility review. Coordinates with FDA on detention, refusal, and redelivery of noncompliant shipments. A border-enforcement/logistics role distinct from FDA's FSVP, which places hazard-analysis and verification duties on the US importer itself.
